ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Lor Mai Gai. Closest parent is a steamed glutinous rice parcel with meat and vegetables. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid code fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed glutinous rice parcel in lotus leaf with chicken, Chinese mushrooms, dried shrimp, and lap cheong (waxed sausage)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00PB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for steamed rice-based parcels; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Lor Mai Gai specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Lor Mai Gai or glutinous rice lotus leaf parcel as of 2026.

serving.description: 1 half-parcel (edible contents; lotus leaf wrapper excluded)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Lor Mai Gai is one half-parcel (approximately 180g of edible contents, excluding the lotus leaf wrapper), as commonly served at Singapore dim sum restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Lor Mai Gai (Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lor Mai Gai. All per-100g values are taken from this verified record. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — glutinous rice (sticky rice) GI ~85–98
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lor Mai Gai as a composite dish. GI of 90 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for glutinous (sticky) rice, which reports GI values of approximately 85–98. Glutinous rice constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per 180g serving calculated as: (90 × 47.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 42.9, rounded to 43.1 per input record. This is among the highest GL values for any common dim sum item.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (580mg/100g; 1044mg per 180g serving) from oyster sauce, soy sauce, dried shrimp, and lap cheong concentrated into the glutinous rice during steaming
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid Lor Mai Gai or limit intake to a maximum of one-quarter parcel (~90g, ~522mg sodium) on rare occasions, as a single standard serving provides approximately 44–52% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~90) from glutinous rice base and very high glycemic load per serving (GL ~43) with minimal dietary fibre (0.5g/100g) to buffer postprandial glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should avoid Lor Mai Gai or strictly limit intake to one-quarter parcel (~90g, GL ~21.6), as the glutinous rice base causes a very rapid and large postprandial blood glucose spike; plain steamed rice, har gow, or vegetable dim sum items are strongly preferred alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (580mg/100g), moderate saturated fat (2.8g/100g) from lap cheong and chicken skin, and cholesterol (42mg/100g) contribute to cardiovascular risk, particularly when consumed in full-parcel portions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Lor Mai Gai to occasional consumption of no more than one-quarter parcel (~90g) due to the combined burden of high sodium, saturated fat from waxed sausage, and the pro-inflammatory effect of the very high glycemic load on vascular endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI glutinous rice base may promote systemic inflammation via rapid glucose response; partially offset by anti-inflammatory beta-glucans (lentinan) and ergothioneine from dried shiitake mushrooms
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Lor Mai Gai in moderation (half-parcel or less), noting that the dried shiitake mushroom component provides some anti-inflammatory benefit, but the high glycemic load and moderate saturated fat from lap cheong remain concerns for systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lor Mai Gai (糯米雞, Nuò Mǐ Jī in Mandarin; also romanized as Lo Mai Gai in Cantonese) is one of the most substantial and filling items in Cantonese dim sum cuisine. It consists of seasoned glutinous (sticky) rice mixed with diced chicken, Chinese dried shiitake mushrooms, dried shrimp, and sliced lap cheong (Chinese waxed sausage), all wrapped tightly in a dried lotus leaf and steamed until the rice is fully cooked and fragrant. The lotus leaf imparts a distinctive subtle earthy and floral aroma to the rice during steaming. When unwrapped at the table, the dark, glistening sticky rice is revealed with its savoury filling embedded within. The texture is dense, chewy, and cohesive — quite unlike regular steamed rice. One half-parcel (approximately 180g) is a common single serving and can constitute a complete meal for some diners. Nutritionally, Lor Mai Gai provides 205 kcal per 100g, with 9.5g protein, 7.8g fat, and 26.5g carbohydrate. It is clinically notable for its very high glycemic index (~90) from the glutinous rice base and very high glycemic load per serving (GL ~43), making it one of the most glycaemically challenging common dim sum items. Sodium content is also very high at 580mg/100g (1044mg per 180g serving) due to the combination of oyster sauce, soy sauce, dried shrimp, and lap cheong. It is dairy-free but contains soy and shellfish allergens. Lor Mai Gai is a staple at Singapore dim sum restaurants and is associated with Cantonese yum cha culture originating from Guangdong (Canton), China.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are verified. GI is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Confidence is high as the exact dish is present in the HPB database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Lor Mai Gai is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite steamed dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source. All other micronutrients are fully populated from the verified HPB source. GI of 90 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI values (85–98) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for Lor Mai Gai as a composite dish exists in the approved GI source. Glycemic load per 180g serving calculated as (90 × 47.7g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 43.1. The lotus leaf wrapper is excluded from the portion weight (180g refers to edible contents only). Lap cheong (Chinese waxed sausage) is not halal-certified; this dish is not suitable for halal dietary requirements. Hindi local name provided as transliteration since no established Hindi culinary term exists for this Cantonese dish.
